text: Welcome to another video. Today we're actually reviewing robotic cholecystectomy.
This is an operation that I think many people use as a nice stepping stone as they build up their robotic experience and we'll highlight some of the nuances to the case.

A sixty-year-old woman with a history of no significant surgical history presented with chronic abdominal pain. She had had multiple ultrasounds performed in the emergency department with gallstones, but no evidence of acute cholecystitis.

She had repeatedly been discharged with referrals to surgeons that had not come to fruition, had a HIDA scan with an EF of less than ten percent. I saw her in my clinic and rescheduled her for a robotic cholecystectomy.
Whenever performing robotic colocystectomy, it's important to recognize that the setup is a little different than laparoscopic procedures.

The surgeon prepares for the robotic cholecystectomy by using all eight millimeter ports, tucking both arms of the patient, positioning them in a twenty percent reverse Trendelenburg, and placing an OG tube.

The surgeon uses a force bipolar for retraction and dissection in the right lower quadrant. A 30-degree camera is positioned in the right paramedian area, while the umbilical port is utilized for hook, colliery, and monopolar scissors, facilitating extraction through this port.
The robotic arm is positioned in the left upper quadrant to retract the gallbladder fundus, facilitating exposure for further dissection.
The surgeon begins the standard dissection of the gallbladder, with minimal visible inflammation in the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneum on the lateral aspect of the artery, mobilizing the artery and elevating the peritoneum higher on the gallbladder beyond the lower edge.
The surgeon begins developing the lateral peritoneal dissection, focusing on the area around the artery and gallbladder.
Both of those maneuvers right there will really help to facilitate us, I think, a better operation.
The surgeon continues dissecting the peritoneum on the lateral aspect of the cystic artery, a technique referred to as the cystic artery mobilization of Erich Naur.
The surgeon continues the dissection underneath the gallbladder on its lateral side, further mobilizing the peritoneum and cystic artery.
The surgeon identifies a twisted cystic duct, initially mistaken for aberrant arterial anatomy, and proceeds with dissection to straighten it out.
The hook cotter is really nice in this setting.

One of the things that it's sort of interesting to get used to with robotic surgery is the lack of the ability to exchange instruments as much as you may have with laparoscopic surgery. And so really becoming more precise and efficient with your dissection will help you to assure that you're able to be efficient and not have to have too many instrument exchanges.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect and remove tissue between the cystic artery and the cystic duct, further mobilizing these structures.
The surgeon continues dissection towards achieving the critical view of safety, preparing to place clips and transect the cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon carefully dissects the tissue off the cystic plate, ensuring precise separation of the gallbladder from the liver bed.
The surgeon extends the dissection higher on the cystic plate, ensuring a safer transection by carefully separating the tissue.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, clearly displaying two structures entering the organ. Clips are applied to these structures before cutting them.
The surgeon uses robotic monopolar scissors from the console to perform the transection, as there is only a scrub tech assisting at the bedside.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully detach the gallbladder from the cystic plate, following extensive prior dissection.
The surgeon undocks the robotic instruments and removes the gallbladder through the umbilical port using a retrieval bag.
laparoscopically patient was discharged same day of course no narcotics used in the recovery phase and felt significantly better after the operation thank you hopefully you enjoyed it
